name: inequality
documentation: Spatial Inequality Analysis in PySAL ===================================== ![tag](https://img.shields.io/github/v/release/pysal/inequality?include_prereleases&sort=semver) [![Continuous Integration](https://github.com/pysal/inequality/actions/workflows/testing.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/pysal/inequality/actions/workflows/testing.yml) [![codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/pysal/inequality/branch/main/graph/badge.svg)](https://codecov.io/gh/pysal/inequality) [![Documentation](https://img.shields.io/static/v1.svg?label=docs&message=current&color=9cf)](http://pysal.org/inequality/) Methods for measuring spatial inequality. Installation ------------ Install inequality from PyPI by running: ``` $ pip install inequality ``` Install inequality from conda-forge by running: ``` $ conda install -c conda-forge inequality ``` #### Requirements - libpysal - numpy - scipy Contribute ---------- PySAL-inequality is under active development and contributors are welcome. If you have any suggestion, feature request, or bug report, please open a new [issue](https://github.com/pysal/inequality/issues) on GitHub. To submit patches, please follow the PySAL development [guidelines](http://pysal.org/docs/devs/) and open a [pull request](https://github.com/pysal/inequality). Once your changes get merged, you’ll automatically be added to the [Contributors List](https://github.com/pysal/inequality/graphs/contributors). Support ------- If you are having issues, please talk to us in the [gitter room](https://gitter.im/pysal/inequality). License ------- The project is licensed under the [BSD license](https://github.com/pysal/giddy/blob/main/LICENSE.txt). Funding ------- Award #1421935 [New Approaches to Spatial Distribution Dynamics](https://www.nsf.gov/awardsearch/showAward?AWD_ID=1421935)

modules.name: inequality
documentation: # {mod}`inequality` --- Spatial Inequality Analysis

modules.name: inequality.theil
documentation: Theil Inequality metrics

classes.name: inequality.theil.Theil
documentation:
Classic Theil measure of inequality.

$$
T = \sum_{i=1}^n
    \left( \frac{y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^n y_i} \ln
        \left[ N \frac{y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^n y_i}\right]
    \right
)
$$

# Parameters

y

: An array in the shape $(n,t)$ or $(n,)$
  with $n$ taken as the observations across which inequality is
  calculated. If `y` is $(n,)$ then a scalar inequality value is
  determined. If `y` is $(n,t)$ then an array of inequality values are
  determined, one value for each column in `y`.

# Attributes

T

: An array in the shape $(t,)$ or $(1,)$
  containing Theil's *T* for each column of `y`.

# Notes

This computation involves natural logs. To prevent `ln[0]` from occurring, a
small value is added to each element of `y` before beginning the computation.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import libpysal
>>> import numpy
>>> from inequality.theil import Theil
```

```pycon
>>> f = libpysal.io.open(libpysal.examples.get_path('mexico.csv'))
>>> vnames = [f'pcgdp{dec}' for dec in range(1940, 2010, 10)]
>>> y = numpy.array([f.by_col[v] for v in vnames]).T
>>> theil_y = Theil(y)
```

```pycon
>>> theil_y.T
array([0.20894344, 0.15222451, 0.10472941, 0.10194725, 0.09560113,
       0.10511256, 0.10660832])
```

classes.name: inequality.theil.TheilD
documentation:
Decomposition of Theil's *T* based on partitioning of
observations into exhaustive and mutually exclusive groups.

# Parameters

y

: An array in the shape $(n,t)$ or $(n,)$
  with $n$ taken as the observations across which inequality is
  calculated. If `y` is $(n,)$ then a scalar inequality value is
  determined. If `y` is $(n,t)$ then an array of inequality values are
  determined, one value for each column in `y`.

partition

: An array in the shape $(n,)$ of elements indicating which partition
  each observation belongs to. These are assumed to be exhaustive.

# Attributes

T

: An array in the shape $(t,)$ or $(1,)$
  containing the global inequality *T*.

bg

: An array in the shape $(n,t)$ or $(n,)$
  representing between group inequality.

wg

: An array in the shape $(n,t)$ or $(n,)$
  representing within group inequality.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import libpysal
>>> import numpy
>>> from inequality.theil import TheilD
```

```pycon
>>> f = libpysal.io.open(libpysal.examples.get_path('mexico.csv'))
>>> vnames = [f'pcgdp{dec}' for dec in range(1940, 2010, 10)]
>>> y = numpy.array([f.by_col[v] for v in vnames]).T
>>> regimes = numpy.array(f.by_col('hanson98'))
>>> theil_d = TheilD(y, regimes)
```

```pycon
>>> theil_d.bg
array([0.0345889 , 0.02816853, 0.05260921, 0.05931219, 0.03205257,
       0.02963731, 0.03635872])
```

```pycon
>>> theil_d.wg
array([0.17435454, 0.12405598, 0.0521202 , 0.04263506, 0.06354856,
       0.07547525, 0.0702496 ])
```

classes.name: inequality.theil.TheilDSim
documentation:
Random permutation based inference on Theil's inequality decomposition.
Provides for computationally based inference regarding the inequality
decomposition using random spatial permutations.
See {cite}`rey_interregional_2010`.

# Parameters

y

: An array in the shape $(n,t)$ or $(n,)$
  with $n$ taken as the observations across which inequality is
  calculated. If `y` is $(n,)$ then a scalar inequality value is
  determined. If `y` is $(n,t)$ then an array of inequality values are
  determined, one value for each column in `y`.

partition

: An array in the shape $(n,)$ of elements indicating which partition
  each observation belongs to. These are assumed to be exhaustive.

permutations

: The number of random spatial permutations for computationally
  based inference on the decomposition.

# Attributes

observed

: An array in the shape $(n,t)$ or $(n,)$
  representing a `TheilD` instance for the observed data.

bg

: An array in the shape `(permutations+1, t)`
  representing between group inequality.

bg_pvalue

: An array in the shape $(t,1)$ representing the $p$-value
  for the between group measure. Measures the percentage of the realized
  values that were greater than or equal to the observed `bg` value.
  Includes the observed value.

wg

: An array in the shape `(permutations+1)`
  representing within group inequality. Depending on the
  shape of `y`, the array may be 1- or 2-dimensional.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import libpysal
>>> import numpy
>>> from inequality.theil import TheilDSim
```

```pycon
>>> f = libpysal.io.open(libpysal.examples.get_path('mexico.csv'))
>>> vnames = [f'pcgdp{dec}' for dec in range(1940, 2010, 10)]
>>> y = numpy.array([f.by_col[v] for v in vnames]).T
>>> regimes = numpy.array(f.by_col('hanson98'))
>>> numpy.random.seed(10)
>>> theil_ds = TheilDSim(y, regimes, 999)
```

```pycon
>>> theil_ds.bg_pvalue
array([0.4  , 0.344, 0.001, 0.001, 0.034, 0.072, 0.032])
```

modules.name: inequality.gini
documentation: Gini based Inequality Metrics

functions.name: inequality.gini._gini
documentation:
Memory efficient calculation of Gini coefficient
in relative mean difference form.

Parameters
----------

x : array-like

Attributes
----------

g : float
    Gini coefficient.

Notes
-----
Based on
http://www.statsdirect.com/help/default.htm#nonparametric_methods/gini.htm.

classes.name: inequality.gini.Gini
documentation:
Classic Gini coefficient in absolute deviation form.

# Parameters

y

: An array in the shape $(n,1)$ containing the attribute values.

# Attributes

g

: Gini coefficient.

classes.name: inequality.gini.Gini_Spatial
documentation:
Spatial Gini coefficient.

Provides for computationally based inference regarding the contribution of
spatial neighbor pairs to overall inequality across a set of regions.
See {cite}`Rey_2013_sea`.

# Parameters

y

: An array in the shape $(n,1)$ containing the attribute values.

w

: Binary spatial weights object.

permutations

: The number of permutations for inference.

# Attributes

g

: Gini coefficient.

wg

: Neighbor inequality component (geographic inequality).

wcg

: Non-neighbor inequality component (geographic complement inequality).

wcg_share

: Share of inequality in non-neighbor component.

p_sim

: (If `permuations > 0`) pseudo $p$-value for spatial gini.

e_wcg

: (If `permuations > 0`) expected value of non-neighbor
  inequality component (level) from permutations.

s_wcg

: (If `permuations > 0`) standard deviation non-neighbor
  inequality component (level) from permutations.

z_wcg

: (If `permuations > 0`) z-value non-neighbor inequality
  component (level) from permutations.

p_z_sim

: (If `permuations > 0`) pseudo $p$-value based on
  standard normal approximation of permutation based values.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import libpysal
>>> import numpy
>>> from inequality.gini import Gini_Spatial
```

Use data from the 32 Mexican States, decade frequency 1940-2010.

```pycon
>>> f = libpysal.io.open(libpysal.examples.get_path('mexico.csv'))
>>> vnames = [f'pcgdp{dec}' for dec in range(1940, 2010, 10)]
>>> y = numpy.transpose(numpy.array([f.by_col[v] for v in vnames]))
```

Define regime neighbors.

```pycon
>>> regimes = numpy.array(f.by_col('hanson98'))
>>> w = libpysal.weights.block_weights(regimes, silence_warnings=True)
>>> numpy.random.seed(12345)
>>> gs = Gini_Spatial(y[:,0], w)
```

```pycon
>>> gs.p_sim
0.04
```

```pycon
>>> gs.wcg
4353856.0
```

```pycon
>>> gs.e_wcg
4170356.7474747472
```

Thus, the amount of inequality between pairs of states that are not in the
same regime (neighbors) is significantly higher than what is expected
under the null of random spatial inequality.

modules.name: inequality._indices
documentation:
Diversity indices as suggested in Nijkamp & Poot (2013) [1] and Nijkamp & Poot
(2015) [2]

# References

[^1] Nijkamp & Poot
[^2] Nijkamp, P. and Poot, J. "Cultural Diversity: A Matter of Measurement".

> IZA Discussion Paper Series No. 8782
> {cite}`nijkamp2015cultural`
> <https://www.econstor.eu/bitstream/10419/107568/1/dp8782.pdf>

functions.name: inequality._indices.abundance
documentation:
Abundance index. {cite}`nijkamp2015cultural`

# Parameters

x

: An $(N, k)$ shaped array containing $N$ rows (one per
  neighborhood) and $k$ columns (one per cultural group).

# Returns

a

: Abundance index.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import numpy
>>> x = numpy.array([[0, 1, 2], [0, 2, 4], [0, 0, 3]])
>>> abundance(x)
2
```

# Returns

# Examples

functions.name: inequality._indices.theil_th
documentation:
Theil index TH as expressed in equation (32) of [2]. {cite}`nijkamp2015cultural`

documentation:

```pycon
>>> y = numpy.random.randint(1, 10, size=(4,3))

array([[6, 1, 4],

```

```pycon
>>> shannon_se(y)
1.094070862104929
```

functions.name: inequality._indices._gini
documentation: Gini for a single row to be used both by `gini_gi` and `gini_gig`.

functions.name: inequality._indices.gini_gi
documentation:
Gini GI index. {cite}`nijkamp2015cultural`

NOTE: based on 3rd eq. of "Calculation" in:

> <http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gini_coefficient>

Returns same value as `gini` method in the R package `reldist` (see
<http://rss.acs.unt.edu/Rdoc/library/reldist/html/gini.html>) if every
category has at least one observation.

# Parameters

x

: An $(N, k)$ shaped array containing $N$ rows (one per
  neighborhood) and $k$ columns (one per cultural group).

# Returns

ggi

: Gini GI index.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import numpy
>>> numpy.random.seed(0)
>>> y = numpy.random.randint(1, 10, size=(4,3))
>>> y
array([[6, 1, 4],
       [4, 8, 4],
       [6, 3, 5],
       [8, 7, 9]])
```

```pycon
>>> round(gini_gi(y), 10)
0.0512820513
```

functions.name: inequality._indices.gini_gig
documentation:
Gini GI index. {cite}`nijkamp2015cultural`

NOTE: based on Wolfram Mathworld formula in:

> <http://mathworld.wolfram.com/GiniCoefficient.html>

# Parameters

x

: An $(N, k)$ shaped array containing $N$ rows (one per
  neighborhood) and $k$ columns (one per cultural group).

# Returns

ggig

: Gini GI index for every group $k$.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import numpy
>>> numpy.random.seed(0)
>>> y = numpy.random.randint(1, 10, size=(4,3))
>>> y
array([[6, 1, 4],
       [4, 8, 4],
       [6, 3, 5],
       [8, 7, 9]])
```

```pycon
>>> gini_gig(y)
array([0.125     , 0.32894737, 0.18181818])
```

functions.name: inequality._indices.gini_gi_m
documentation:
Gini GI index (equivalent to `gini_gi`, not vectorized).

NOTE: based on Wolfram Mathworld formula in:

Returns same value as `gini_gi`.

# Parameters

x

: An $(N, k)$ shaped array containing $N$ rows (one per
  neighborhood) and $k$ columns (one per cultural group).

# Returns

ggim

: Gini GI index.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import numpy
>>> numpy.random.seed(0)
>>> y = numpy.random.randint(1, 10, size=(4,3))
>>> y
array([[6, 1, 4],
       [4, 8, 4],
       [6, 3, 5],
       [8, 7, 9]])
```

```pycon
>>> round(gini_gi_m(y), 10)
0.0512820513
```

functions.name: inequality._indices.hoover_hi
documentation:
Hoover index HI. {cite}`nijkamp2015cultural`

NOTE: based on

> <http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hoover_index>

# Parameters

: An $(N, k)$ shaped array containing $N$ rows (one per
  neighborhood) and $k$ columns (one per cultural group).

# Returns

hhi

: Hoover HI index.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import numpy
>>> numpy.random.seed(0)
>>> y = numpy.random.randint(1, 10, size=(4,3))
```

functions.name: inequality._indices.similarity_w_wd
documentation:
Similarity weighted diversity. {cite}`nijkamp2015cultural`

# Parameters

x

: An $(N, k)$ shaped array containing $N$ rows (one per
  neighborhood) and $k$ columns (one per cultural group).

tau

: A $(k, k)$ array where $tau_{ij}$ represents dissimilarity
  between group $i$ and group $j$. Diagonal elements are
  assumed to be one.

# Returns

swwd

: Similarity weighted diversity index.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import numpy
>>> numpy.random.seed(0)
>>> y = numpy.random.randint(1, 10, size=(4,3))
>>> y
array([[6, 1, 4],
       [4, 8, 4],
       [6, 3, 5],
       [8, 7, 9]])
```

```pycon
>>> numpy.random.seed(0)
>>> tau = numpy.random.uniform(size=(3,3))
>>> numpy.fill_diagonal(tau, 0.)
>>> tau = (tau + tau.T)/2
>>> tau
array([[0.        , 0.63003627, 0.52017529],
       [0.63003627, 0.        , 0.76883356],
       [0.52017529, 0.76883356, 0.        ]])
```

```pycon
>>> round(similarity_w_wd(y, tau), 10)
0.581859634
```

functions.name: inequality._indices.segregation_gsg
documentation:
Segregation index GS.

This is a Duncan&Duncan index of a group against the rest combined.

# Parameters

x

: An $(N, k)$ shaped array containing $N$ rows (one per
  neighborhood) and $k$ columns (one per cultural group).

# Returns

sgsg

: An array with GSg indices for the $k$ groups.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import numpy
>>> numpy.random.seed(0)
>>> y = numpy.random.randint(1, 10, size=(4,3))
>>> y
array([[6, 1, 4],
       [4, 8, 4],
       [6, 3, 5],
       [8, 7, 9]])
```

```pycon
>>> segregation_gsg(y).round(6)
array([0.182927, 0.24714 , 0.097252])
```

functions.name: inequality._indices.modified_segregation_msg
documentation:
Modified segregation index GS.

This is a modified version of GSg index as used by Van Mourik et al. (1989)
{cite}`van_Mourik_1989`.

# Parameters

x

: An $(N, k)$ shaped array containing $N$ rows (one per
  neighborhood) and $k$ columns (one per cultural group).

# Returns

ms_inds

: An array with MSg indices for the $k$ groups.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import numpy
>>> numpy.random.seed(0)
>>> y = numpy.random.randint(1, 10, size=(4,3))
>>> y
array([[6, 1, 4],
       [4, 8, 4],
       [6, 3, 5],
       [8, 7, 9]])
```

```pycon
>>> modified_segregation_msg(y).round(6)
array([0.085207, 0.102249, 0.04355 ])
```

functions.name: inequality._indices.isolation_isg
documentation:
Isolation index IS. {cite}`nijkamp2015cultural`

# Parameters

x

: An $(N, k)$ shaped array containing $N$ rows (one per

# Returns

# Examples

```pycon
       [4, 8, 4],

       [8, 7, 9]])
```

```pycon
>>> isolation_isg(y).round(6)
array([1.07327 , 1.219953, 1.022711])
```

functions.name: inequality._indices.isolation_ii
documentation:
Isolation index $II_g$ as in equation (23) of [2].
{cite}`nijkamp2015cultural`

# Parameters

x

: An $(N, k)$ shaped array containing $N$ rows (one per
  neighborhood) and $k$ columns (one per cultural group).

# Returns

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import numpy
>>> numpy.random.seed(0)
>>> y = numpy.random.randint(1, 10, size=(4,3))
>>> y
array([[6, 1, 4],
       [4, 8, 4],
       [6, 3, 5],
       [8, 7, 9]])
```

```pycon
>>> isolation_ii(y).round(6)
array([1.11616 , 1.310804, 1.03433 ])
```

functions.name: inequality._indices.ellison_glaeser_egg
documentation:
Ellison and Glaeser (1997) {cite}`ellison_1997` index of concentration.
Implemented as in equation (5) of original reference.

# Parameters

x

: An $(N, k)$ shaped array containing $N$ rows (one per
  area) and $k$ columns (one per industry). Each cell indicates
  employment figures for area $n$ and industry $k$.

hs

: An array of dimension $(k,)$ containing the Herfindahl
  indices of each industry's plant sizes. If not passed, it is
  assumed every plant contains one and only one worker and thus
  $H_k = 1 / P_k$, where $P_k$ is the total
  employment in $k$.

# Returns

eg_inds

: EG index for each of the $k$ groups.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import numpy
>>> numpy.random.seed(0)
>>> z = numpy.random.randint(10, 50, size=(3,4))
>>> z
array([[10, 13, 13, 49],
       [19, 29, 31, 46],
       [33, 16, 34, 34]])
```

```pycon
>>> ellison_glaeser_egg(z).round(6)
array([0.054499, 0.016242, 0.010141, 0.028803])
```

```pycon
>>> numpy.random.seed(0)
>>> v = numpy.random.uniform(0, 1, size=(4,)).round(3)
>>> v
array([0.549, 0.715, 0.603, 0.545])
```

```pycon
>>> ellison_glaeser_egg(z, hs=v).round(6)
array([-1.06264 , -2.39227 , -1.461383, -1.117953])
```

# References

- {cite}`ellison_1997` Ellison, G. and Glaeser, E. L. "Geographic Concentration in U.S. Manufacturing Industries: A Dartboard Approach". Journal of Political Economy. 105: 889-927

functions.name: inequality._indices.ellison_glaeser_egg_pop
documentation:
Ellison and Glaeser (1997) {cite}`ellison_1997` index of concentration.
Implemented to be computed with data about people (segregation/diversity)
rather than as industry concentration, following Mare et al (2012)
{cite}`care_2012`.

# Parameters

x

: An $(N, k)$ shaped array containing $N$ rows (one per
  neighborhood) and $k$ columns (one per cultural group).

# Returns

eg_inds

: EG index for each of the $k$ groups.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import numpy
>>> numpy.random.seed(0)
>>> y = numpy.random.randint(1, 10, size=(4,3))
>>> y
array([[6, 1, 4],
       [4, 8, 4],
       [6, 3, 5],
       [8, 7, 9]])
```

```pycon
>>> ellison_glaeser_egg_pop(y).round(6)
array([-0.021508,  0.013299, -0.038946])
```

# References

- {cite}`ellison_1997` – Ellison, G. and Glaeser, E. L. "Geographic Concentration in U.S. Manufacturing Industries: A Dartboard Approach". Journal of Political Economy. 105: 889-927
- {cite}`care_2012` – Care, D., Pinkerton, R., Poot, J. and Coleman, A. (2012) "Residential sorting across Auckland neighbourhoods." New Zealand Population Review, 38, 23-54.

functions.name: inequality._indices.maurel_sedillot_msg
documentation:
Maurel and Sedillot (1999) {cite}`maurel_1999` index of concentration.
Implemented as in equation (7) of original reference.

# Parameters

x

: An $(N, k)$ shaped array containing $N$ rows (one per
  area) and $k$ columns (one per industry). Each cell indicates
  employment figures for area $n$ and industry $k$.

hs

: An array of dimension $(k,)$ containing the Herfindahl
  indices of each industry's plant sizes. If not passed, it is
  assumed every plant contains one and only one worker and thus
  $H_k = 1 / P_k$, where $P_k$ is the total
  employment in $k$.

# Returns

ms_inds

: MS index for each of the $k$ groups.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import numpy
>>> numpy.random.seed(0)
>>> z = numpy.random.randint(10, 50, size=(3,4))
>>> z
array([[10, 13, 13, 49],
       [19, 29, 31, 46],
       [33, 16, 34, 34]])
```

```pycon
>>> maurel_sedillot_msg(z).round(6)
array([ 0.078583,  0.035977,  0.039374, -0.009049])
```

```pycon
>>> numpy.random.seed(0)
>>> v = numpy.random.uniform(0, 1, size=(4,)).round(3)
>>> v
array([0.549, 0.715, 0.603, 0.545])
```

```pycon
>>> maurel_sedillot_msg(z, hs=v).round(6)
array([-1.010102, -2.324216, -1.38869 , -1.200499])
```

# References

- {cite}`maurel_1999` – Maurel, F. and Sédillot, B. (1999). "A Measure of the Geographic Concentration in French Manufacturing Industries." Regional Science and Urban Economics 29: 575-604.

# References
